questTitle: O-Level Chemistry: Chemical Bonding - Ionic and Covalent Compounds Worksheet 1
subject: Chemistry
description: Master chemical bonding! This worksheet covers the formation of ionic and simple covalent compounds, their structures, types of attractive forces (strong electrostatic forces vs weak intermolecular forces), electrical conductivity, and comparing bonding in compounds with polyatomic ions. Show clear understanding of concepts!

questions.question: What is an ionic bond?
options: A bond formed by sharing of electrons between two non-metal atoms
A bond formed by the electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions
A bond formed by the attraction between metal atoms and delocalised electrons
A bond formed by weak intermolecular forces
questions.answer: A bond formed by the electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: A bond formed by the transfer of electrons from a metal to a non-metal
A bond formed by the attraction between positive and negative ions
A bond formed by the sharing of one or more pairs of electrons between atoms
A bond formed by weak forces between molecules
questions.answer: A bond formed by the sharing of one or more pairs of electrons between atoms

questions.question: Ionic bonds typically form between:
options: Two non-metal atoms
Two metal atoms
A metal atom and a non-metal atom
Two noble gas atoms
questions.answer: A metal atom and a non-metal atom

questions.question: Covalent bonds typically form between:
options: A metal atom and a non-metal atom
Two metal atoms
Two non-metal atoms
A metal atom and a noble gas atom
questions.answer: Two non-metal atoms

questions.question: When sodium (Na) reacts with chlorine (Cl) to form sodium chloride (NaCl), what happens to the electrons?
options: Sodium and chlorine share electrons equally
Sodium gains one electron from chlorine
Sodium loses one electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions
Both atoms lose electrons
questions.answer: Sodium loses one electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions

questions.question: Why does sodium form a Na⁺ ion when forming ionic compounds?
options: Sodium gains one electron to achieve a stable noble gas configuration
Sodium loses one electron from its outermost shell to achieve a stable noble gas configuration (like neon)
Sodium shares its one valence electron
Sodium needs to gain seven electrons to be stable
questions.answer: Sodium loses one electron from its outermost shell to achieve a stable noble gas configuration (like neon)

questions.question: Why does chlorine form a Cl⁻ ion when forming ionic compounds?
options: Chlorine loses one electron to achieve stability
Chlorine loses seven electrons to achieve stability
Chlorine gains one electron to achieve a stable noble gas configuration (like argon)
Chlorine shares one electron with another atom
questions.answer: Chlorine gains one electron to achieve a stable noble gas configuration (like argon)

questions.question: When magnesium (Mg) reacts with oxygen (O) to form magnesium oxide (MgO), what ions are formed?
options: Mg⁺ and O⁻
Mg²⁺ and O²⁻
Mg⁻ and O⁺
Mg²⁻ and O²⁺
questions.answer: Mg²⁺ and O²⁻

questions.question: In the formation of calcium chloride (CaCl₂), calcium loses 2 electrons. How are these electrons distributed?
options: Both electrons go to one chlorine atom
Each chlorine atom gains one electron
The electrons are shared between calcium and chlorine
The electrons are lost to the surroundings
questions.answer: Each chlorine atom gains one electron

questions.question:
Which electron configuration changes correctly represent the formation of sodium chloride?

Na: 2,8,1 and Cl: 2,8,7
What is the structure of ionic compounds in the solid state?
options: Simple molecular structure with molecules arranged randomly
Giant ionic lattice structure with ions arranged in a regular, repeating 3D pattern
Simple atomic structure with atoms closely packed
Chain-like structure with ions arranged in lines
questions.answer: Giant ionic lattice structure with ions arranged in a regular, repeating 3D pattern

questions.question: In the giant ionic lattice of sodium chloride, how are the ions arranged?
options: Sodium ions are only surrounded by sodium ions
Each sodium ion is surrounded by chloride ions, and each chloride ion is surrounded by sodium ions
Ions are arranged randomly throughout the structure
Sodium and chloride ions are arranged in separate layers
questions.answer: Each sodium ion is surrounded by chloride ions, and each chloride ion is surrounded by sodium ions

questions.question: What type of force holds the ions together in an ionic lattice?
options: Weak intermolecular forces of attraction
Covalent bonds between ions
Strong electrostatic forces of attraction between oppositely charged ions
Magnetic forces between ions
questions.answer: Strong electrostatic forces of attraction between oppositely charged ions

questions.question: Why do ionic compounds have high melting and boiling points?
options: Because they have weak intermolecular forces that are easy to overcome
Because a large amount of energy is needed to overcome the strong electrostatic forces of attraction between the oppositely charged ions
Because the ions are very small
Because they contain covalent bonds within the lattice
questions.answer: Because a large amount of energy is needed to overcome the strong electrostatic forces of attraction between the oppositely charged ions

questions.question: Magnesium oxide (MgO) has a higher melting point than sodium chloride (NaCl). Which explanation is correct?
options: MgO has weaker electrostatic forces than NaCl
Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ have higher charges than Na⁺ and Cl⁻, resulting in stronger electrostatic forces of attraction
MgO has covalent bonds while NaCl has ionic bonds
NaCl has a giant lattice structure while MgO does not
questions.answer: Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ have higher charges than Na⁺ and Cl⁻, resulting in stronger electrostatic forces of attraction

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 3: Formation of Simple Covalent Compounds
questions.question: In a molecule of chlorine (Cl₂), how are the two chlorine atoms bonded?
options: By transfer of electrons from one chlorine to another
By sharing one pair of electrons (single covalent bond)
By electrostatic attraction between Cl⁺ and Cl⁻ ions
By weak intermolecular forces
questions.answer: By sharing one pair of electrons (single covalent bond)

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 3: Formation of Simple Covalent Compounds
questions.question: In a molecule of oxygen (O₂), the two oxygen atoms are bonded by a double bond. What does this mean?
options: The atoms share one pair of electrons
The atoms share two pairs of electrons
The atoms share three pairs of electrons
The atoms transfer two electrons
questions.answer: The atoms share two pairs of electrons

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 3: Formation of Simple Covalent Compounds
questions.question: In a molecule of nitrogen (N₂), the two nitrogen atoms are bonded by a triple bond. How many electron pairs are shared?
options: One pair
Two pairs
Three pairs
Four pairs
questions.answer: Three pairs

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 3: Formation of Simple Covalent Compounds
questions.question: In a water molecule (H₂O), how many covalent bonds does the oxygen atom form?
options: One single bond
Two single bonds
One double bond
Three single bonds
questions.answer: Two single bonds

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 3: Formation of Simple Covalent Compounds
questions.question: Why do atoms form covalent bonds?
options: To lose electrons and become positively charged
To gain electrons and become negatively charged
To achieve a stable noble gas electron configuration by sharing electrons
To create free-moving electrons
questions.answer: To achieve a stable noble gas electron configuration by sharing electrons

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 3: Formation of Simple Covalent Compounds ⭐
questions.question: In a molecule of ammonia (NH₃), nitrogen has 5 valence electrons and each hydrogen has 1 valence electron. How many bonding pairs and lone pairs of electrons are around the nitrogen atom?
options: 4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs
3 bonding pairs, 1 lone pair
2 bonding pairs, 2 lone pairs
1 bonding pair, 3 lone pairs
questions.answer: 3 bonding pairs, 1 lone pair

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Structure of Simple Covalent Compounds
questions.question: Simple covalent compounds exist as:
options: Giant ionic lattices
Giant covalent structures
Simple molecules
Metallic lattices
questions.answer: Simple molecules

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Structure of Simple Covalent Compounds
questions.question: Within a simple covalent molecule like H₂O, what type of bond holds the atoms together?
options: Weak intermolecular forces
Ionic bonds
Strong covalent bonds
Electrostatic forces between ions
questions.answer: Strong covalent bonds

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Structure of Simple Covalent Compounds
questions.question: Between simple covalent molecules, what type of force exists?
options: Strong covalent bonds
Strong ionic bonds
Weak intermolecular forces of attraction
Strong electrostatic forces
questions.answer: Weak intermolecular forces of attraction

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Structure of Simple Covalent Compounds
questions.question: Why do simple covalent compounds generally have low melting and boiling points?
options: Because strong covalent bonds between molecules need to be broken
Because only weak intermolecular forces of attraction between molecules need to be overcome, which requires little energy
Because they have giant lattice structures
Because they contain ions that can move freely
questions.answer: Because only weak intermolecular forces of attraction between molecules need to be overcome, which requires little energy

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Structure of Simple Covalent Compounds
questions.question: When ice melts to form water, what happens?
options: Covalent bonds within water molecules are broken
Intermolecular forces between water molecules are overcome
Ionic bonds are broken
Oxygen-hydrogen bonds are broken
questions.answer: Intermolecular forces between water molecules are overcome

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Structure of Simple Covalent Compounds ⭐
questions.question:
Compare the bonds broken when:
(a) Sodium chloride melts
(b) Ice melts

Which statement is correct?
options: Both involve breaking covalent bonds
Both involve breaking ionic bonds
(a) involves overcoming strong electrostatic forces between ions; (b) involves overcoming weak intermolecular forces between molecules
(a) involves overcoming weak forces; (b) involves overcoming strong forces
questions.answer: (a) involves overcoming strong electrostatic forces between ions; (b) involves overcoming weak intermolecular forces between molecules

questions.question: Can solid ionic compounds conduct electricity?
options: Yes, because they contain ions
Yes, because they contain free electrons
No, because the ions are held in fixed positions in the lattice and cannot move
No, because they do not contain any charged particles
questions.answer: No, because the ions are held in fixed positions in the lattice and cannot move

questions.question: Can molten ionic compounds conduct electricity?
options: No, because all the ions are destroyed when melted
No, because there are no charged particles
Yes, because the ions are free to move and carry charge
Yes, because free electrons are released when melted
questions.answer: Yes, because the ions are free to move and carry charge

questions.question: Can ionic compounds dissolved in water conduct electricity?
options: No, because water destroys the ions
No, because the ions become neutral in water
Yes, because the ions dissociate and are free to move in solution
Yes, because water provides free electrons
questions.answer: Yes, because the ions dissociate and are free to move in solution

questions.question: Solid sodium chloride does not conduct electricity, but molten sodium chloride does. Explain why in terms of structure and bonding.
options: In solid NaCl, there are no ions; in molten NaCl, ions are formed
In solid NaCl, ions are in fixed positions and cannot move; in molten NaCl, ions are free to move and carry charge
In solid NaCl, covalent bonds prevent conduction; in molten NaCl, covalent bonds break
In solid NaCl, electrons cannot move; in molten NaCl, electrons are released
questions.answer: In solid NaCl, ions are in fixed positions and cannot move; in molten NaCl, ions are free to move and carry charge

questions.question: Can simple covalent compounds conduct electricity in any state?
options: Yes, in the solid state only
Yes, when molten or dissolved in water
No, because they do not contain ions or free electrons to carry charge
Yes, because they contain shared electrons
questions.answer: No, because they do not contain ions or free electrons to carry charge

questions.question: Why does pure water (a covalent compound) not conduct electricity?
options: Because water molecules are too small
Because water contains no ions or free electrons; it consists of neutral molecules
Because water is a liquid
Because the covalent bonds in water are too strong
questions.answer: Because water contains no ions or free electrons; it consists of neutral molecules

questions.question:
Compare the electrical conductivity of sodium chloride and methane (CH₄).

Which statement is correct?
options: Both conduct electricity when molten
Neither conducts electricity in any state
NaCl conducts when molten or in aqueous solution (mobile ions); CH₄ does not conduct in any state (no ions or free electrons)
CH₄ conducts when dissolved in water; NaCl does not conduct
questions.answer: NaCl conducts when molten or in aqueous solution (mobile ions); CH₄ does not conduct in any state (no ions or free electrons)

questions.question: What is a polyatomic ion?
options: An ion made of a single atom
A group of covalently bonded atoms that carries an overall charge
A neutral molecule
An ion formed by a single metal atom
questions.answer: A group of covalently bonded atoms that carries an overall charge

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a polyatomic ion?
options: Na⁺
Cl⁻
SO₄²⁻ (sulfate ion)
Mg²⁺
questions.answer: SO₄²⁻ (sulfate ion)

questions.question: In the sulfate ion (SO₄²⁻), what type of bonding exists between the sulfur atom and the oxygen atoms?
options: Ionic bonding
Covalent bonding
Metallic bonding
Intermolecular forces
questions.answer: Covalent bonding

questions.question: In the ammonium ion (NH₄⁺), the nitrogen atom is bonded to four hydrogen atoms. What type of bonding holds these atoms together?
options: Ionic bonding
Covalent bonding
Electrostatic forces between ions
Weak intermolecular forces
questions.answer: Covalent bonding

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 6: Polyatomic Ions - Compounds with Polyatomic Ions
questions.question: In sodium sulfate (Na₂SO₄), what types of bonding are present?
options: Only ionic bonding
Only covalent bonding
Ionic bonding between Na⁺ and SO₄²⁻; covalent bonding within the SO₄²⁻ ion
Only intermolecular forces
questions.answer: Ionic bonding between Na⁺ and SO₄²⁻; covalent bonding within the SO₄²⁻ ion

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 6: Polyatomic Ions - Compounds with Polyatomic Ions
questions.question: In ammonium chloride (NH₄Cl), what types of bonding are present?
options: Only covalent bonding throughout
Only ionic bonding throughout
Covalent bonding within the NH₄⁺ ion; ionic bonding between NH₄⁺ and Cl⁻
Only weak intermolecular forces
questions.answer: Covalent bonding within the NH₄⁺ ion; ionic bonding between NH₄⁺ and Cl⁻

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 6: Polyatomic Ions - Compounds with Polyatomic Ions ⭐
questions.question: Ammonium sulfate ((NH₄)₂SO₄) contains both ammonium ions and sulfate ions. Describe all the types of bonding present in this compound.
options: Only ionic bonding
Only covalent bonding
Covalent bonding within NH₄⁺ ions, covalent bonding within SO₄²⁻ ions, and ionic bonding between NH₄⁺ and SO₄²⁻ ions
Ionic bonding within NH₄⁺, ionic bonding within SO₄²⁻, and covalent bonding between them
questions.answer: Covalent bonding within NH₄⁺ ions, covalent bonding within SO₄²⁻ ions, and ionic bonding between NH₄⁺ and SO₄²⁻ ions

questions.question: Sodium chloride has a melting point of 801°C while hydrogen chloride has a melting point of -114°C. Why is there such a large difference?
options: NaCl has weak ionic bonds; HCl has strong covalent bonds
NaCl has strong electrostatic forces between ions requiring much energy to overcome; HCl has weak intermolecular forces between molecules requiring little energy to overcome
Both have the same type of bonding but different molecular sizes
HCl is a gas so it naturally has a lower melting point
questions.answer: NaCl has strong electrostatic forces between ions requiring much energy to overcome; HCl has weak intermolecular forces between molecules requiring little energy to overcome

questions.question: Carbon dioxide (CO₂) is a gas at room temperature while sodium oxide (Na₂O) is a solid with a very high melting point. Which explanation is correct?
options: CO₂ has stronger bonds than Na₂O
CO₂ is a simple covalent molecule with weak intermolecular forces; Na₂O is an ionic compound with strong electrostatic forces between ions
Na₂O has weak ionic bonds; CO₂ has strong covalent bonds
Both are ionic but Na₂O has larger ions
questions.answer: CO₂ is a simple covalent molecule with weak intermolecular forces; Na₂O is an ionic compound with strong electrostatic forces between ions

questions.question:
Which substance would you expect to have the highest melting point?

A: Methane (CH₄) - simple covalent
B: Sodium chloride (NaCl) - ionic
C: Water (H₂O) - simple covalent
D: Ammonia (NH₃) - simple covalent
options: A: Methane
B: Sodium chloride
C: Water
D: Ammonia
questions.answer: B: Sodium chloride

questions.question: Calcium oxide (CaO) has a higher melting point than sodium chloride (NaCl). Both are ionic compounds. What is the reason for this difference?
options: CaO has weaker electrostatic forces
Ca²⁺ and O²⁻ have higher charges than Na⁺ and Cl⁻, leading to stronger electrostatic forces of attraction
CaO has covalent character while NaCl is purely ionic
NaCl has a larger lattice structure
questions.answer: Ca²⁺ and O²⁻ have higher charges than Na⁺ and Cl⁻, leading to stronger electrostatic forces of attraction

questions.question:
Compare the properties of sodium sulfate (Na₂SO₄) and sulfur dioxide (SO₂).

Which statement correctly describes both compounds?
options: Both are ionic compounds with high melting points
Both are simple covalent compounds with low melting points
Na₂SO₄ is ionic with high melting point and conducts when molten; SO₂ is simple covalent with low melting point and does not conduct
Na₂SO₄ is simple covalent; SO₂ is ionic
questions.answer: Na₂SO₄ is ionic with high melting point and conducts when molten; SO₂ is simple covalent with low melting point and does not conduct

questions.question:
A student tests substance X and finds:
- It has a high melting point
- It does not conduct electricity as a solid
- It conducts electricity when dissolved in water

What type of substance is X most likely to be?
options: A simple covalent compound
An ionic compound
A metal
A non-polar covalent compound
questions.answer: An ionic compound

questions.question:
A student tests substance Y and finds:
- It has a low boiling point
- It does not conduct electricity in any state
- It is insoluble in water

What type of substance is Y most likely to be?
options: An ionic compound
A metal
A simple covalent compound
A compound containing polyatomic ions
questions.answer: A simple covalent compound

questions.question:
Potassium nitrate (KNO₃) has the following properties:
- High melting point
- Conducts electricity when molten or dissolved in water
- Contains both K⁺ and NO₃⁻ ions

What types of bonding are present, and what holds the compound together?
options: Only covalent bonding; weak intermolecular forces hold it together
Only ionic bonding between K and N and O atoms
Covalent bonding within NO₃⁻ ion; ionic bonding (strong electrostatic forces) between K⁺ and NO₃⁻ ions holds the compound together
Ionic bonding within NO₃⁻ ion; covalent bonding between K⁺ and NO₃⁻
questions.answer: Covalent bonding within NO₃⁻ ion; ionic bonding (strong electrostatic forces) between K⁺ and NO₃⁻ ions holds the compound together

questions.question:
A student is given three substances:

1. Calcium chloride (CaCl₂)
2. Tetrachloromethane (CCl₄)
3. Ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃)

Which row correctly describes the bonding and conductivity of each substance?
options: All three are ionic and conduct electricity when molten
1: Ionic, conducts when molten; 2: Simple covalent, does not conduct; 3: Contains both ionic and covalent bonds, conducts when molten
1: Covalent, does not conduct; 2: Ionic, conducts when molten; 3: Ionic, does not conduct
All three are covalent and do not conduct electricity
questions.answer: 1: Ionic, conducts when molten; 2: Simple covalent, does not conduct; 3: Contains both ionic and covalent bonds, conducts when molten
